name: TAM
description: Proposed by Fred Davis in his 1986 PhD dissertation, published formally in 1989. One of the most cited models in information systems research. Adapted TRA to address user acceptance of information systems. Posits that perceived usefulness (PU) and perceived ease of use (PEOU) directly affect behavioural intention (BI). Explains why users decide to adopt a technology. Focuses on pre-adoption or early adoption.
notes:
Core constructs:
• Perceived usefulness: a user's subjective probability that using the system will increase his or her job performance.
• Perceived ease of use: the degree to which a user expects use of the system to be free of effort.
• Behavioural intention

Limitations: Davis did not measure subjective norms (outside influences). Initial study did not find that attitude had a significant effect on behavioural intentions.

keyCitations.title: Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and User Acceptance of Information Technology

name: ECT
description: Originally proposed by Richard Oliver in 1977–1980 in consumer behaviour research. Explains how post-purchase satisfaction and repurchase intentions depend on pre-purchase expectations and subsequent experience with products or services. Satisfaction is fuelled by belief disconfirmation from evaluating performance against expectations. Explains why consumers purchase again.
notes: Steps: (1) Formation of expectation — consumers form initial expectations prior to purchase. (2) Interaction and experience — they use the product and form perceptions of performance. (3) Confirmation or disconfirmation — they assess perceived performance against expectations (positive or negative). (4) Cognitive adjustment — they form a level of satisfaction. Flow: Users form expectations → experience the product → confirm (or disconfirm) expectations → this shapes satisfaction → which shapes continuance intention → which drives actual continued use.

keyCitations.title: A Cognitive Model of the Antecedents and Consequences of Satisfaction Decisions

name: ECM
description: Built by Bhattacherjee in 2001, adapting ECT specifically to information systems. Well-respected in IS/MIS. Explains why users continue using a technology after initial adoption. Focuses on post-adoption behaviour. Replaced repurchase intention in ECT with IS continuance intention. Confirmation is defined as congruence between expectation and actual performance; performance construct removed as its influence is captured by confirmation. Perceived usefulness from TAM was introduced to reflect post-acceptance expectations.
notes: Users' intention to continue IS usage depends on three factors: (1) satisfaction with IS — stronger effect on continuance than PU; (2) degree of confirmation of expectations; (3) post-usage perceived usefulness — cognitive belief. Initial expectations and confirmation after first use are key antecedents to satisfaction. Model focuses on post-usage and post-acceptance variables to explain usage continuance.

keyCitations.title: Understanding Information Systems Continuance: An Expectation-Confirmation Model

name: ECM-IS
description: Full flow: Prior Expectations shape Confirmation; Confirmation influences Perceived Usefulness and Satisfaction; both drive Continuance Intention and ultimately Continued Use. Perceived Usefulness also has a direct path to Continuance Intention.

keyCitations.title: Understanding Information Systems Continuance: An Expectation-Confirmation Model

notes: Full flow from prior expectations through confirmation, perceived usefulness, satisfaction, continuance intention, and continued use. PU has a direct path to continuance intention (bypassing satisfaction).

name: TRA
description: Precursor of TAM. A person's performance of a specified behaviour is determined by behavioural intention (BI) to perform the behaviour. Behavioural intention is jointly determined by attitude (A) and subjective norms (SN) toward the behaviour: BI = A + SN.
notes: Foundational theory linking attitudes and social norms to intention and behaviour.

keyCitations.title: Belief, Attitude, Intention, and Behavior: An Introduction to Theory and Research

name: TPB
description: Extends TRA by adding perceived behavioural control. Decomposed theory of planned behaviour: intention is determined by attitude, subjective norms, and perceived behavioural control; intention and PBC predict behaviour.
notes: Addresses behaviours that are not under full volitional control.

keyCitations.title: The theory of planned behavior

name: TAM 2
description: Extends TAM by introducing social influencing processes and cognitive instrumental processes. Perceived Usefulness (PU) is influenced by social factors (subjective norm, image), cognitive instrumental factors (job relevance, output quality, result demonstrability), experience, and perceived ease of use (PEOU). PEOU is influenced by experience. Behavioral intention to use (BI) is influenced by subjective norm, voluntariness, PU, and PEOU; BI in turn leads to actual system use.
notes: Social influencing (light red in diagram): Subjective norm (influences PU and BI); Image (influences PU); Voluntariness (influences BI). Cognitive instrumental (light purple): Job relevance, Output quality, Result demonstrability (all influence PU); Perceived Usefulness; Perceived Ease of Use (influenced by Experience; influences PU and BI). Experience influences both PU and PEOU. Final path: BI → Actual system use.

keyCitations.title: A Theoretical Extension of the Technology Acceptance Model: Four Longitudinal Field Studies

name: UTAUT
description: Developed by Venkatesh and colleagues in 2003; consolidates TAM and several other models. Widely respected in IS research. Original UTAUT was validated primarily in organisational settings where technology use was mandatory or semi-mandatory.
notes: In mandatory contexts, social influence works differently (managerial pressure). Extended to UTAUT2 (2012) for voluntary, consumer contexts.

keyCitations.title: User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view

name: UTAUT2
description: Extension of UTAUT for voluntary, consumer contexts. Venkatesh added hedonic motivation, price value, and habit so the model fits contexts where consumers choose freely whether to adopt. More appropriate for voluntary adoption than UTAUT.
notes: UTAUT (2003) was organisational/mandatory; UTAUT2 addresses criticism that social influence and other factors work differently when adoption is voluntary.

keyCitations.title: Consumer Acceptance and Use of Information Technology: Extending the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology

name: TTF
description: A theory developed by Goodhue and Thompson (1995). Technology is more likely to have a positive impact on performance when the capabilities of the technology match the tasks the user needs to perform. It is about the fit between task requirements and technology functionality. Task characteristics and technology characteristics jointly determine task-technology fit; fit influences utilization and performance impact.
notes: Core idea: fit between what the task demands and what the technology provides. When fit is high, users are more likely to use the technology (utilization) and to experience better performance outcomes. TTF is itself a measurable construct.

keyCitations.title: Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance

name: DOI
description: Originated with Everett Rogers in 1962 (Diffusion of Innovations, 1st edition). One of the most cited theories in social science, respected across multiple fields. The 2003 citation is the 5th edition. Describes how innovations spread, how adopters are categorised by stage of adoption, and how perceived characteristics of an innovation—relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, and observability (visibility of results)—shape adoption through the innovation-decision process. Information systems research often adds constructs such as image and result demonstrability alongside these Rogers attributes.
notes:
Five adopter types: innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, laggards.

Five stages of the innovation-decision process (Rogers, 1995): (1) Knowledge — gaining information about the innovation; (2) Persuasion — forming an attitude toward it; (3) Decision — deciding to adopt or reject; (4) Implementation — putting it into use; (5) Confirmation — reinforcing or reversing the decision (continuation or discontinuation).

Perceived innovation attributes (shown in the diagram feeding Persuasion): relative advantage, compatibility, observability (how visible results are to others), image (social prestige of use), and result demonstrability (how easy outcomes are to show—related to TAM2). Together with knowledge, these shape persuasion and downstream stages.

keyCitations.title: Diffusion of Innovations
Diffusion of Innovations (5th ed.)

name: CJM
description: Concept emerged in the 1990s–2000s from service design and marketing practice; practitioner framework rather than a single academic theory. Meyer & Schwager (2007) in Harvard Business Review helped popularise it academically. Maps the sequence of customer experiences from first contact to full description of the service (Micheaux & Bosio, 2019).
notes: A customer journey is a series of direct and indirect contacts or interactions with a product or service (Meyer & Schwager, 2007), which may be positive, negative, or neutral (Berry et al., 2002). Experience at each touchpoint affects perceived relationship quality and customer commitment (Palmatier et al., 2006).

keyCitations.title: Understanding Customer Experience
Customer journey mapping as a new way to teach data-driven marketing

name: TAJM
description: Used to document the adoption experience of innovators and early adopters, forming the basis for developing the adoption journey map for the early majority. CJM is fundamental for the development of TAJM — applies customer journey mapping to technology adoption.
notes: Bridges DOI adopter segments with journey mapping; builds on CJM for technology adoption contexts.

name: My Thesis Model
description: Thesis model linking product discovery quality (your contribution) to continuance intention through confirmation, perceived usefulness, and satisfaction. Continuance intention leads to actual continued usage as an optional outcome.
notes: Independent variable: Product Discovery Quality (your contribution). Mediators: Confirmation, Perceived Usefulness, Satisfaction. Dependent variable: Continuance Intention. Optional outcome: Actual Continued Usage.

name: My Thesis 2
description: Integrates Task-Technology Fit (TTF) with the Expectation-Confirmation Model of IS Continuance (ECM-IS). When a digital agritech tool fits the tasks farmers or agribusiness users need to perform (TTF), they are more likely to continue using it (Continuance Intention). TTF adds a functional fit dimension that ECM alone does not fully capture. The research question: Does task-technology fit influence not only initial utilization but also long-term continuance intention? Context: Agritech in Sub-Saharan Africa — an emerging, underresearched setting where SSA-specific factors (low digital literacy, connectivity, trust, switching costs) may strengthen, weaken, or disrupt the TTF → CI relationship.
notes: Theoretical logic: Fit drives satisfaction and continued engagement. Utilization (TTF) ≠ Continuance Intention (ECM): utilization is actual behavior; CI is forward-looking intention. TTF was designed for adoption/usage phase; ECM for post-adoption. CI predicts sustained adoption — critical in agritech where tool abandonment is a real problem. Potential mediators: Satisfaction (bridging TTF and ECM). Potential moderators: Digital literacy, infrastructure quality, Trust. SSA boundary conditions: data costs, connectivity, social norms, low literacy may disrupt fit-continuance link. Defensible framing: The TTF → CI relationship is theoretically logical but empirically unverified in agritech in SSA, where unique contextual factors may strengthen, weaken, or disrupt this relationship.

keyCitations.title: Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
Understanding Information Systems Continuance: An Expectation-Confirmation Model

name: Expectancy-Value Model of Attitudes
description: The expectancy-value model of attitudes explains how attitudes are formed from beliefs about outcomes (expectancies) and the evaluation/importance of those outcomes. In Fishbein and Ajzen’s framework, individuals combine salient behavioral beliefs with outcome evaluations to form an overall attitude that influences behavioural intention (via TRA-style logic).
notes:
Conceptual flow: salient beliefs about outcomes (expectancies) weighted by evaluations/importance → attitude toward the behavior → behavioural intention.

Note: in this app, the model is represented using the available constructs; belief/evaluation components are not stored as separate constructs yet.

keyCitations.title: Belief, attitude, intention, and behavior: An introduction to theory and research

name: Information System Success Model
description: The DeLone and McLean Information Systems Success Model explains what makes an information system successful. The original framework (1992) defined interdependent success dimensions; the ten-year update (2003) added service quality, intention to use, and consolidated consequences into net benefits. System quality, information quality, and service quality shape use and intention to use; these behaviours and perceptions feed user satisfaction; satisfaction and use contribute to net benefits for users and organizations.
notes:
Core constructs (updated model):
• System quality — technical performance of the IS (e.g. reliability, responsiveness).
• Information quality — quality of outputs (e.g. accuracy, relevance, completeness).
• Service quality — quality of support surrounding the system (e.g. help desk, training).
• Use / intention to use — actual use and willingness to use the system.
• User satisfaction — affective response to the IS experience.
• Net benefits — net positive impacts at individual or organizational level (e.g. efficiency, effectiveness, innovation); replaces separate impact constructs in the 2003 formulation.

Causal ordering is often depicted as qualities → use and intention → satisfaction → net benefits, with additional paths from qualities to satisfaction in many empirical specifications.

keyCitations.title: Information Systems Success: The Quest for the Dependent Variable
The DeLone and McLean Model of Information Systems Success: A Ten-Year Update

name: Technology–Organization–Environment Framework
description: The Technology–Organization–Environment (TOE) framework explains technology adoption at the organizational level. It focuses on three contexts: the technology context (characteristics of the innovation or system), the organization context (e.g. size, resources, managerial and technical capabilities), and the environment context (e.g. regulation, competitive pressure, infrastructure and institutional support). These contexts jointly influence whether and how organizations adopt and assimilate technology.
notes:
Three TOE contexts (typical content):
• Technology context → system characteristics (e.g. relative advantage, compatibility, complexity).
• Organization context → size, resources, capabilities (including managerial and structural readiness).
• Environment context → regulation, competition, infrastructure, and broader institutional forces.

———
Relation to this thesis (ECM + TTF + facilitating conditions)

You are building a model that combines:
• User perception (ECM)
• Task alignment (TTF)
• Context constraints (facilitating conditions)

TOE helps explain where those constraints come from — that is powerful, but indirect.

• Your study focuses on individual users (farmers, technicians) and continuance intention (CI).
• TOE focuses on organizations (firms, institutions) and adoption decisions.

So there is a level mismatch:
• TOE = organizational level
• Your model = individual level

Even though it is not a core model in your thesis, TOE is useful for one thing: explaining contextual constraints.

Especially in Sub-Saharan Africa, a plausible conceptual bridge is:

Environment (TOE)
  → Facilitating conditions (your model)
  → Continuance intention

Examples of what flows through that chain: connectivity, affordability, institutional support, infrastructure.

This aligns with the idea that satisfaction alone is not enough — conditions must enable use.

keyCitations.title: The Processes of Technological Innovation
The Process of Innovation Assimilation in Organizations: An International Perspective

name: Social Cognitive Theory
description: Social cognitive theory (SCT) is one of the most influential accounts of human behaviour, emphasizing reciprocal interactions among personal factors (e.g. self-efficacy and beliefs), behaviour, and the environment. In information systems, Compeau and Higgins (1995) applied and extended SCT to computer utilization, positing that outcome expectations (performance and personal), self-efficacy, affect, and anxiety relate to how people use computers at work; Compeau et al. (1999) further developed longitudinal insights into these relationships.
notes:
Core constructs in the MIS stream (Compeau & Higgins):
• Outcome expectations (performance) — performance-related consequences of use.
• Outcome expectations (personal) — personal/self-relevant consequences of use.
• Self-efficacy — confidence in ability to perform computing tasks.
• Affect — emotional response toward using computers.
• Anxiety — apprehension about computer use (often computer anxiety).

Behavioural outcome: utilization (or related use measures) is often predicted by self-efficacy and outcome expectations; anxiety and affect are typically important antecedents or correlates.

keyCitations.title: Social Foundations of Thought and Action: A Social Cognitive Theory
Computer Self-Efficacy: Development of a Measure and Initial Test
Social Cognitive Theory and Individual Reactions to Computing Technology: A Longitudinal Study

name: Model of PC Utilization
description: Derived from Triandis’s (1977) theory of interpersonal behavior, the Model of PC Utilization (MPCU) offers a competing perspective to the Theory of Reasoned Action and Theory of Planned Behavior by emphasizing habits, affect, and perceived consequences alongside social and cognitive determinants. Thompson, Higgins, and Howell (1991) adapted Triandis’s structure to information systems and used it to predict personal computer utilization among knowledge workers. Although the original study emphasized usage behavior rather than intention, the framework applies broadly to individual acceptance and use of information technologies.
notes:
Core determinants in the Thompson et al. (1991) stream include job fit, complexity, long-term consequences, affect toward use, and social factors.

• Compared with TRA/TPB: MPCU draws on Triandis rather than Fishbein–Ajzen, and bundles cognitive, affective, and social antecedents in one model.

• Your thesis framing: Thompson et al. (1991) predicted usage; the diagram below shows these determinants leading to **behavioral intention**, which aligns with testing intention as the outcome while keeping theory’s roots.

For a behavioral outcome, link **Utilization** (or equivalent) instead of or in addition to intention.

keyCitations.title: Interpersonal Behavior
Personal Computing: Toward a Conceptual Model of Utilization

name: Motivational Model
description: A substantial body of psychological research treats motivation as a central explanation for behaviour. Vallerand (1997) synthesizes the core ideas of intrinsic versus extrinsic motivation and related mechanisms. Within information systems, Davis, Bagozzi, and Warshaw (1992) applied motivational theory to new technology adoption and use, modeling how intrinsic and extrinsic motivation to use computers shape beliefs such as perceived usefulness and perceived enjoyment, which in turn influence usage intentions. Venkatesh and Speier (1999) and related work extend motivation-based reasoning in IS contexts such as training and adoption.
notes:
The classic IS operationalization (Davis et al., 1992) positions **intrinsic motivation** and **extrinsic motivation** as antecedents of **perceived usefulness** and **perceived enjoyment**, with intention (and ultimately use) as downstream outcomes.

• Ties to TAM: PU and enjoyment bridge motivation to acceptance; extended TAM and UTAUT2 often incorporate hedonic or intrinsic mechanisms.

• Psychology base: self-determination and cognitive evaluation traditions (e.g. Deci & Ryan) underpin how intrinsic and extrinsic reasons are distinguished.

keyCitations.title: Toward a Hierarchical Model of Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivation
Extrinsic and Intrinsic Motivation to Use Computers in the Workplace
Computer Technology Training in the Workplace: A Longitudinal Investigation of the Effect of Mood
